refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication packet loss at or below the customer-defined threshold (acceptance criterion: ≤1% under agreed network conditions) to ensure safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-009 addresses a critical quality-of-service requirement for teleoperation. Packet loss directly impacts command latency, feedback fidelity, and operator situational awareness—all safety-critical factors in remote heavy equipment control. Refinement clarifies that the requirement is conditional on 'agreed network conditions' and ties acceptance to a measurable, customer-negotiated target rather than a fixed value.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct packet loss measurement under simulated and live network conditions (WiFi, cellular, hybrid) matching customer deployment scenarios. Measure end-to-end loss across the teleoperation link (operator console → vehicle → operator console). Acceptance: measured packet loss ≤ customer-defined limit for ≥95% of test runs.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall implement error detection and recovery mechanisms to minimize packet loss impact on command execution.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall monitor and report real-time network quality metrics (packet loss, latency, jitter) to the operator.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall adapt communication protocol parameters (compression, retransmission, redundancy) based on detected network conditions.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement adaptive packet prioritization and selective retransmission in the teleoperation protocol stack, with fallback to reduced-fidelity telemetry if packet loss exceeds threshold.
DP-B: Deploy redundant communication channels (dual-path routing, multi-carrier aggregation) with automatic failover to maintain packet loss below threshold.
DP-C: Implement forward error correction (FEC) and packet aggregation at the application layer, combined with network quality monitoring and operator alerts.
DP-D: Use a hybrid approach combining adaptive protocol tuning (DP-A), selective redundancy (DP-B), and application-layer FEC (DP-C) with real-time network diagnostics.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a multi-layered packet loss mitigation strategy combining (1) adaptive protocol tuning with selective retransmission and compression, (2) selective redundancy for critical commands via dual-path routing, (3) forward error correction and packet aggregation at the application layer, and (4) continuous network quality monitoring with operator alerts and graceful degradation modes.

potential_sources: DP-D couples teleoperation protocol design (network layer) with vehicle command execution logic (application layer) and operator interface (HMI). This is acceptable because packet loss mitigation inherently spans these domains.

DP-D couples network infrastructure assumptions (availability of dual paths, carrier support) with software implementation. Risk: if dual-path routing is unavailable in customer deployment, FEC and adaptive tuning must compensate.

DP-D couples safety criticality (high) with network quality variability. Mitigation: graceful degradation modes and operator alerts ensure safety is maintained even if packet loss approaches threshold.
coupling_check.notes: Coupling is justified by the nature of the requirement. No decoupling opportunity identified without sacrificing effectiveness. Recommend explicit interface contracts between protocol layer, application layer, and HMI to manage coupling.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined packet loss threshold is achievable with standard commercial network infrastructure (WiFi 6, LTE, or hybrid). RISK: If threshold is <0.5%, may require premium network services or proprietary solutions.
ASSUMPTION: Vehicle and operator console hardware support dual-path routing and FEC processing. RISK: Legacy equipment may lack computational capacity for real-time FEC.
ASSUMPTION: Operator is trained to interpret network quality alerts and adjust behavior accordingly. RISK: Untrained operators may ignore alerts or misinterpret graceful degradation.
ASSUMPTION: Network conditions remain relatively stable during a single teleoperation session. RISK: Rapid network degradation (e.g., sudden interference) may exceed mitigation response time.
ASSUMPTION: Packet loss is the primary network quality issue; latency and jitter are secondary. RISK: High latency (>500ms) may be more limiting for operator control than packet loss.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly define (1) the adaptive tuning algorithm and its decision thresholds, (2) the FEC scheme and overhead budget, (3) the dual-path routing strategy and failover logic, and (4) the operator alert protocol and graceful degradation modes. Recommend a separate DP for network condition monitoring and diagnostics to avoid overloading the primary DP.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end teleoperation communication stack, including protocol adaptation, packet loss mitigation (FEC, retransmission, redundancy), network quality monitoring, and operator interface for network diagnostics. Scope includes software running on operator console and vehicle-side teleoperation gateway, but excludes underlying network infrastructure (Network Link) and vehicle command execution (Vehicle module).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands (movement, tool control, mode selection) from operator console HMI. Teleoperation module encodes, prioritizes, and transmits via Network Link.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Sends vehicle state, sensor data, and video feed to operator console. Teleoperation module compresses, prioritizes, and transmits via Network Link. Includes network quality metrics (packet loss, latency, jitter).

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional communication channel (WiFi, cellular, hybrid). Teleoperation module interfaces with Network Link to transmit/receive packets and monitor link quality.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Sends alerts to Safety Services if packet loss exceeds threshold or network quality degrades below safe operating limits. Enables Safety Services to trigger operator notifications or autonomous safety actions.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation module is the natural owner of packet loss mitigation logic, protocol adaptation, and network quality monitoring. All core DP-D functions (FEC, retransmission, redundancy, adaptive tuning) are contained within this module. Minimal diffusion: only SafetyAlert interface crosses boundary to Safety Services.

Vehicle module owns command execution and vehicle state management, but packet loss mitigation is not a vehicle-specific concern. Assigning DP-D to Vehicle would scatter protocol logic across vehicle firmware and operator console, creating high diffusion and maintenance burden.

Safety Services owns safety policy enforcement and alerts, but packet loss mitigation is a communication quality concern, not a safety policy. Assigning DP-D to Safety Services would conflate network quality with safety logic, reducing clarity and reusability.

NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. While it could theoretically own all sub-modules, assigning DP-D to NGHE Solution would bypass the Teleoperation module and create architectural ambiguity. Teleoperation is the appropriate sub-module.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of DP-D because (1) it already owns the teleoperation protocol stack and operator-vehicle communication, (2) packet loss mitigation is a communication quality concern, not a vehicle or safety policy concern, (3) containment score is 95%, indicating minimal diffusion, and (4) external interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, Network Link, SafetyAlert) are well-defined and manageable.
containment_risks: RISK: Teleoperation module may become complex if FEC, adaptive tuning, and redundancy logic are all implemented in a single module. MITIGATION: Decompose DP-D into sub-modules (FEC engine, adaptive protocol tuner, redundancy manager) within Teleoperation, each with clear interfaces.

RISK: Network Link interface may not provide sufficient visibility into link quality metrics (packet loss, latency, jitter) for Teleoperation to make adaptive decisions. MITIGATION: Define explicit Network Link interface contract that includes real-time quality metrics.

RISK: SafetyAlert interface may create a tight coupling between Teleoperation and Safety Services. If Safety Services changes alert protocol, Teleoperation must adapt. MITIGATION: Define SafetyAlert as a publish-subscribe interface to decouple Teleoperation from Safety Services implementation.

RISK: Operator console and vehicle-side Teleoperation implementations may diverge if not carefully synchronized. MITIGATION: Use a shared protocol specification and version control to ensure consistency.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm that Network Link interface provides real-time packet loss and latency metrics. If not, request enhancement to Network Link to expose these metrics. Define SafetyAlert as a publish-subscribe interface to reduce coupling. Consider decomposing Teleoperation into sub-modules (FEC engine, adaptive tuner, redundancy manager, network monitor) to manage complexity.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D directly addresses the refined FR by implementing multiple packet loss mitigation strategies (adaptive tuning, FEC, redundancy, monitoring) that collectively work to maintain packet loss ≤ customer-defined threshold. DP-D is neither over-specified (it does not prescribe implementation details like specific FEC codes) nor under-specified (it clearly identifies the four key mechanisms). Coupling is justified and acceptable.

diffusion_risks: DIFFUSION RISK: DP-D logic spans operator console and vehicle-side implementations. If not carefully coordinated, protocol adaptation decisions may diverge between console and vehicle, causing synchronization failures. MITIGATION: Enforce a single protocol specification and version control.

DIFFUSION RISK: Network quality monitoring (part of DP-D) requires data from Network Link module. If Network Link does not expose sufficient metrics, Teleoperation may need to infer quality from packet arrival patterns, adding complexity. MITIGATION: Confirm Network Link interface contract includes packet loss and latency metrics.

DIFFUSION RISK: SafetyAlert interface couples Teleoperation to Safety Services. If Safety Services alert protocol changes, Teleoperation must adapt. MITIGATION: Use publish-subscribe pattern to decouple.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves 95% containment score for DP-D. All core packet loss mitigation logic (FEC, retransmission, redundancy, adaptive tuning) is contained within Teleoperation. Only SafetyAlert interface crosses boundary, which is appropriate for safety-critical systems. No significant diffusion identified.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of FR-NGHE-009 is feasible via network simulation and live testing. Teleoperation module can be tested in isolation by injecting controlled packet loss and measuring mitigation effectiveness. Acceptance criterion (packet loss ≤ customer-defined threshold) is measurable and objective.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation of FR-NGHE-009 to Teleoperation module is clear. Teleoperation owns the mechanisms (FEC, adaptive tuning, redundancy, monitoring) that directly control packet loss. Vehicle module and Safety Services have supporting roles (command execution, safety alerts) but are not responsible for packet loss mitigation.

recommended_revisions: Confirm Network Link interface contract includes real-time packet loss and latency metrics. If not, request enhancement.
Define SafetyAlert interface as publish-subscribe to reduce coupling between Teleoperation and Safety Services.
Decompose Teleoperation module into sub-modules (FEC engine, adaptive tuner, redundancy manager, network monitor) to manage complexity and improve testability.

Establish a protocol specification document that defines the adaptive tuning algorithm, FEC scheme, redundancy strategy, and operator alert protocol. This document should be shared between operator console and vehicle-side implementations.
Plan for sub-level decomposition of FR-NGHE-009 into FR-NGHE-009.1, 009.2, and 009.3 to address error recovery, network monitoring, and adaptive tuning separately.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-009 is not atomic and requires further decomposition. The refined FR identifies three sub-FRs (error recovery, network monitoring, adaptive tuning) that must be individually designed and verified. DP-D is sound and well-allocated to Teleoperation module, but its complexity warrants sub-level analysis to ensure each mechanism (FEC, retransmission, redundancy, adaptive tuning) is properly designed and integrated. Proceeding to the next decomposition level will clarify implementation details, interface contracts, and verification strategies.
decision.justification_detail: The refined FR explicitly identifies three sub-FRs that represent distinct functional concerns: (1) error detection and recovery (FR-NGHE-009.1), (2) network quality monitoring (FR-NGHE-009.2), and (3) adaptive communication (FR-NGHE-009.3). Each sub-FR requires its own DP and IM assignment. Additionally, DP-D combines four mechanisms (adaptive tuning, FEC, redundancy, monitoring) that could be designed and tested independently. Decomposition will improve clarity, testability, and maintainability.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall implement error detection and recovery mechanisms to minimize packet loss impact on command execution.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall monitor and report real-time network quality metrics (packet loss, latency, jitter) to the operator.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall adapt communication protocol parameters (compression, retransmission, redundancy) based on detected network conditions.

assumptions: Customer-defined packet loss threshold is achievable with standard commercial network infrastructure (WiFi 6, LTE, or hybrid).
Vehicle and operator console hardware support dual-path routing and FEC processing.
Operator is trained to interpret network quality alerts and adjust behavior accordingly.
Network conditions remain relatively stable during a single teleoperation session.
Packet loss is the primary network quality issue; latency and jitter are secondary.
Network Link interface provides real-time packet loss and latency metrics to Teleoperation module.
SafetyAlert interface is available for Teleoperation to notify Safety Services of network quality degradation.
open_risks: If customer-defined packet loss threshold is <0.5%, may require premium network services or proprietary solutions beyond standard commercial offerings.
Legacy vehicle hardware may lack computational capacity for real-time FEC and adaptive protocol tuning.
Rapid network degradation (e.g., sudden interference) may exceed mitigation response time, causing temporary packet loss spikes above threshold.
High latency (>500ms) may be more limiting for operator control than packet loss, but is not explicitly addressed in FR-NGHE-009.
Operator console and vehicle-side Teleoperation implementations may diverge if not carefully synchronized via shared protocol specification.
Network Link interface may not expose sufficient quality metrics for Teleoperation to make informed adaptive decisions.
evidence_needed: Network simulation results showing packet loss mitigation effectiveness under various network conditions (WiFi, cellular, hybrid, degraded).
Live testing results in customer deployment scenarios confirming packet loss ≤ customer-defined threshold.
Protocol specification document defining adaptive tuning algorithm, FEC scheme, redundancy strategy, and operator alert protocol.
Network Link interface contract confirming availability of real-time packet loss and latency metrics.
SafetyAlert interface specification defining alert protocol and integration with Safety Services.
Operator training materials and usability testing results confirming operators can interpret network quality alerts and adjust behavior.
Hardware capability assessment confirming vehicle and operator console support FEC and adaptive protocol tuning.
Latency analysis confirming that packet loss mitigation does not introduce unacceptable latency increases.
